title: Architecture Patterns
description: MVVM, MVI, Repository pattern, UiState, UseCases, and architecture at scale

references.title: Android Developers — Guide to App Architecture

lesson:
Android Architecture Guide

Google recommends MVVM with Unidirectional Data Flow: state flows down (ViewModel → UI), events flow up (UI → ViewModel). This makes your app predictable, testable, and maintainable.

The Architecture Layers

  • UI Layer — Composables observe ViewModel state
  • ViewModel — Holds UI state, handles business logic, survives config changes
  • Domain Layer (optional) — UseCase/Interactor classes for reusable business logic
  • Repository — Single source of truth, abstracts data sources
  • Data Sources — Network (Retrofit), local (Room), preferences (DataStore)
UI Layer Domain Layer (optional) Data Layer ┌──────────┐ ┌───────────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │Composable│────▶│ UseCase │────▶│ Repository │ │ │◀────│ (operator invoke) │◀────│ │ └──────────┘ └───────────────────┘ └──────┬───────┘ │ │ ▼ ┌─────┴─────┐ ┌──────────┐ │ │ │ViewModel │ DataSource DataSource │(StateFlow│ (Network) (Local DB) │ <UiState>│ └──────────┘ State flows DOWN ↓ Events flow UP ↑

UiState Pattern

Represent screen state as a sealed class: Loading, Success(data), Error(message). The ViewModel exposes a single StateFlow<UiState>, and the UI handles each case with when.

MVI (Model-View-Intent)

MVI extends UDF by formalizing user actions as Intents (not Android Intents!) and processing them through a Reducer that produces new state. This makes state transitions explicit and debuggable.

// 1. Define user actions as sealed class sealed class HomeIntent { object LoadData : HomeIntent() data class Search(val query: String) : HomeIntent() data class ToggleFavorite(val itemId: String) : HomeIntent() } // 2. Reducer produces new state from current state + intent fun reduce(state: HomeState, intent: HomeIntent): HomeState = when (intent) { is HomeIntent.LoadData -> state.copy(isLoading = true) is HomeIntent.Search -> state.copy(query = intent.query) is HomeIntent.ToggleFavorite -> state.copy( items = state.items.map { if (it.id == intent.itemId) it.copy(isFav = !it.isFav) else it } ) }
MVVM vs MVI: MVVM exposes multiple methods on ViewModel (search(), refresh()). MVI funnels all actions through a single onIntent() entry point. MVI is better for complex screens with many interactions; MVVM is simpler for straightforward screens.

UseCase / Interactor Pattern

The Domain Layer sits between ViewModel and Repository. Each UseCase encapsulates one piece of reusable business logic. Google's convention: a single public operator fun invoke() method.

// Convention: verb + noun + UseCase class GetFilteredArticlesUseCase( private val articlesRepository: ArticlesRepository, private val userPrefsRepository: UserPrefsRepository ) { operator fun invoke(): Flow<List<Article>> { return combine( articlesRepository.getArticles(), userPrefsRepository.getPrefs() ) { articles, prefs -> articles.filter { it.topic in prefs.followedTopics } } } } // ViewModel calls it like a function: val articles = getFilteredArticlesUseCase()
When NOT to use UseCases: If a UseCase simply delegates to a single repository method without adding logic, it's unnecessary boilerplate. Only create UseCases when they combine data from multiple repositories or contain business logic shared across ViewModels.

Architecture at Scale

Architecture patterns change as apps grow. Key considerations:

  • Small app (1-3 devs): MVVM + Repository is sufficient. Domain layer is optional overhead.
  • Medium app (5-15 devs): Add UseCases for shared logic. Modularize into feature modules.
  • Large app (20+ devs): Full Clean Architecture with strict layer boundaries. Feature modules with independent navigation. Shared core modules. Convention plugins for build config.
Key Principle — SSOT (Single Source of Truth): Every data type should have exactly one owner responsible for mutations. For user data, it's UserRepository. For UI state, it's the ViewModel. Multiple sources of truth = inconsistent state = bugs.

questions.title: Recommended UI Architecture
questions.description: Which architecture pattern does Google officially recommend for Android UI development?
options: MVC (Model-View-Controller)
MVP (Model-View-Presenter)
MVVM with Unidirectional Data Flow
Flux/Redux
questions.explanation: Google recommends MVVM with Unidirectional Data Flow (UDF). State flows down (ViewModel → UI), events flow up (UI → ViewModel). This is the natural fit for Jetpack Compose.
hints: Check the official Android architecture guide

questions.title: ViewModel Context Leak
questions.description: This ViewModel crashes on configuration change. Find and fix the memory leak.

checks.message: Use Application context instead of Activity context
Use AndroidViewModel or inject Application context

questions.explanation: ViewModels survive configuration changes, but Activities are destroyed and recreated. Holding an Activity reference causes a memory leak. Use AndroidViewModel with Application context instead.
hints: ViewModels outlive Activities across configuration changes
What type of Context lives as long as the app process?

questions.title: UiState Sealed Class
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to create a sealed class representing three data-loading states.

blanks.hint: Keyword before object for proper equals/hashCode

questions.explanation: A sealed class restricts subclasses to the same file, enabling exhaustive when. The three-state pattern (Loading, Success, Error) is standard for async data in Android.
hints: This keyword ensures all subclasses are known at compile time

questions.title: Repository Pattern
questions.description: Explain how this repository decides where to get data from.
options: Always fetches from network first, falling back to cache on error
Emits cached data immediately, then fetches fresh data — offline-first strategy
Only uses cache, never makes network requests
Fetches from network and cache in parallel
questions.explanation: This implements offline-first / cache-then-network: 1) Emit cached data for fast UI. 2) Fetch fresh data from API. 3) Save to Room and emit again. 4) If network fails and cache exists, silently handle it.
hints: Follow the order of operations: what gets emitted first?

questions.title: Dependency Injection
questions.description: Why is dependency injection (DI) important in Android development?
options: It makes apps run faster by pre-loading all classes
It decouples components, making code testable and swappable (e.g., fake repository in tests)
It automatically handles memory management
It's required by the Android framework
questions.explanation: DI means a class receives its dependencies from outside rather than creating them internally. This makes classes testable (inject fakes in tests), swappable (different implementations for debug/release), and loosely coupled. Hilt is Android's recommended DI framework, built on Dagger.
hints: How do you test a ViewModel that creates its own Repository internally?
Think about swapping real implementations for test fakes

questions.title: Collecting StateFlow in Compose
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to safely collect a ViewModel's StateFlow in a Composable.
questions.explanation: collectAsState() converts a StateFlow into Compose State, triggering recomposition on changes. collectAsStateWithLifecycle() is even better — it stops collecting when the UI is not visible, saving resources. when on a sealed class handles all cases exhaustively.
hints: There is a Compose extension to convert Flows to State
Sealed classes enable exhaustive branching without else

questions.title: Flow Collection Without Lifecycle
questions.description: This code wastes resources by continuing to collect the flow even when the app is in the background. Fix it.
questions.explanation: collectAsState() collects continuously, even when the app is backgrounded. For resource-intensive flows (location, sensor data), use collectAsStateWithLifecycle() which automatically stops collection when the lifecycle drops below STARTED. This saves battery and CPU. Requires androidx.lifecycle:lifecycle-runtime-compose.
hints: What happens to GPS collection when the user switches to another app?
There is a lifecycle-aware version of collectAsState

questions.title: Hilt Module
questions.description: Write a Hilt module that provides a Retrofit instance and a UserRepository. The Retrofit base URL should be "https://api.example.com/" and the repository needs both the API service and a DAO.
questions.explanation: Hilt modules tell the DI framework how to create instances. @Module marks the class, @InstallIn(SingletonComponent) scopes it to the app. @Provides functions return instances. @Singleton ensures one instance app-wide. Hilt automatically passes dependencies between providers.
hints: Each @Provides function returns one dependency
Hilt automatically resolves the parameter chain: Retrofit → UserApi → UserRepository

questions.title: MVI Reducer Pattern
questions.description: Trace what happens when a user dispatches ProfileIntent.ToggleNotifications. What is the resulting state?
options: ProfileState(name = "Alice", notificationsEnabled = true, isLoading = false)
ProfileState(name = "Alice", notificationsEnabled = false, isLoading = false)
ProfileState(name = "", notificationsEnabled = false, isLoading = false)
ProfileState(name = "Alice", notificationsEnabled = false, isLoading = true)
questions.explanation: The reducer uses state.copy(notificationsEnabled = !state.notificationsEnabled). Since notificationsEnabled was true, it flips to false. All other fields stay the same thanks to copy(). This is pure MVI: the reducer is a pure function that takes current state + intent and returns new state.
hints: copy() keeps unchanged fields and only modifies the ones you specify
The ! operator flips a boolean

questions.title: UseCase with operator invoke
questions.description: Write a GetTopArticlesUseCase that combines data from an ArticlesRepository and UserRepository. It should return the top 10 articles sorted by date, filtered to only include articles from authors the user follows.

checks.message: UseCase should have operator fun invoke()
Should combine data from both repositories
Should filter and sort the articles

questions.explanation: A UseCase encapsulates reusable business logic. The operator fun invoke() convention lets callers use it like a function: getTopArticlesUseCase(). This UseCase combines data from two repositories — exactly when the domain layer adds value. Naming convention: verb + noun + UseCase.
hints: Use combine() to merge two Flows
operator fun invoke() lets you call the class like a function

questions.title: When to Add a Domain Layer
questions.description: In Google's architecture guide, when should you add the optional Domain layer (UseCases)?
options: Always — every ViewModel should go through a UseCase
When business logic is shared between multiple ViewModels, or when you want to simplify complex ViewModels
Only in Clean Architecture — MVVM doesn't use UseCases
When you need to cache data between the ViewModel and Repository
questions.explanation: The domain layer is optional. Add UseCases when: (1) Business logic is shared across multiple ViewModels, (2) A ViewModel is becoming too complex and you want to extract logic. Don't add UseCases that simply delegate to a single repository method — that's unnecessary boilerplate.
hints: Think about when a UseCase adds value vs when it's just a pass-through

questions.title: MVI Intent-Reducer in ViewModel
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to complete an MVI-style ViewModel with a reducer.

blanks.hint: Pure function that produces new state

questions.explanation: MVI funnels all user actions through a single entry point (onIntent), which calls a pure reduce function. The reducer uses when on the sealed intent class to produce new state. _state.update { } ensures thread-safe state mutations. This pattern makes state transitions explicit and testable.
hints: The entry point receives an Intent and delegates to the reducer
The reducer is a pure function: (state, intent) → newState

questions.title: Exposed MutableStateFlow
questions.description: This ViewModel violates the UDF principle by exposing mutable state to the UI. Fix it so only immutable state is visible.
questions.explanation: In UDF, the ViewModel is the single source of truth for UI state. Exposing MutableStateFlow lets the UI mutate state directly, breaking unidirectional flow. Use the backing property pattern: private val _state = MutableStateFlow(...) + val state = _state.asStateFlow(). This ensures only the ViewModel can write state.
hints: The naming convention is _privateFlow (mutable) + publicFlow (immutable)
asStateFlow() wraps MutableStateFlow as read-only StateFlow
